nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still tributaries, flooded forest streams, and blackwater creeks of the upper Amazon basin; soft, leaf-littered sandy substrates with moderate detritus and dappled light through overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: dried leaf litter (catappa, oak) to mimic natural detritus layer
driftwood and twisted roots for cover
dim to moderate lighting with floating plants or broad-leaf plants for shade

diet.notes: Scavenges the substrate for small invertebrates, worms, and organic detritus in the wild. In the aquarium readily accepts sinking micro-pellets, high-quality catfish wafers, and frozen/thawed foods such as bloodworms, tubifex, daphnia, and brine shrimp. Blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber) and spirulina-enriched wafers round out the diet. Feed at or after lights-out to ensure corydoras receive food before more competitive tankmates. Do not rely on them as sole tank cleaners.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (neons, cardinals, ember tetras)
other Corydoras species of similar size
avoidWith: nippy species that target barbels or fins
very high-current setups incompatible with slow-water Amazon biotope

imageCandidates.notes: Frank M. Greco; 600x297. Live aquarium specimen clearly showing the diagnostic black dorsal-fin saddle, reticulated flank pattern and sharp snout. Only qualifying CC live photograph; low resolution and slight through-glass haze are the caveats.

summary: The Blacktop Corydoras (Corydoras acutus) is a small Amazonian armored catfish, reaching around 5 cm, recognised by the bold black saddle across the dorsal fin base. It is sometimes confused with C. ellisae and C. amapaensis, which share a similar saddle pattern. Generally considered easy to keep and best maintained in groups of six or more on a fine sand substrate.
careNotes: Corydoras acutus is a straightforward, rewarding species well suited to the community aquarium. Fine sand is essential — coarse gravel abrades the sensitive barbels and ventral skin, leading to bacterial infections that are difficult to cure. A minimum group of six is strongly recommended, as isolated individuals are stressed and less active; groups of eight or more display the most natural foraging and shoaling behavior. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral, reflecting the species’ upper-Amazon origin. Good filtration and regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) are important; while corydoras are tolerant of some variation, they are sensitive to ammonia and nitrite spikes and to dissolved oxygen depletion. A tight-fitting lid is advisable — like all corydoras, C. acutus makes brief trips to the surface to swallow air (intestinal respiration) and can occasionally jump. Avoid copper-based medications; catfish in the family Callichthyidae are notably copper-sensitive. Taxonomic note: a 2024 paper by Dias and Britto proposed reclassifying much of the genus Corydoras into a resurrected Hoplisoma; under that proposal this species would become Hoplisoma acutus. The reclassification is widely discussed but not yet universally adopted in the hobby. C. acutus is most often confused with C. ellisae and C. amapaensis, which share the black dorsal saddle; C. acutus can be distinguished by its sharper snout profile (reflected in the epithet 'acutus') and subtle differences in the extent and shape of the saddle patch.
breedingNotes: Breeding is achievable in a mature, well-maintained aquarium and follows the typical corydoras T-position spawning sequence. Condition a group (2–3 males per female) with frequent water changes and varied live/frozen foods. Spawning is often triggered by a partial water change with slightly cooler water, simulating the onset of the rainy season. Females hold 2–4 adhesive eggs in their cupped pelvic fins before pressing them to a cleaned flat surface (glass, broad leaves, filter inlet). Remove eggs to a separate hatching container with gentle aeration; eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 24°C. Fry are free-swimming within a further 2–3 days and accept micro-worms, infusoria, and crushed fry powder.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras acutus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~4.5–5 cm SL), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–179 ppm, native range (upper Amazon tributaries, Brazil/Peru/Colombia), fine-sand substrate requirement, grouping minimum, diet, breeding notes, and confusion species (C. ellisae, C. amapaensis).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras acutus
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (max ~5.5 cm TL), native range, temperature range, and diet classification (omnivore / benthic scavenger). Confirmed venomous pectoral and dorsal spines noted in genus description.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Corydoras genus-level care guide covering sand substrate requirement, group size (6+), copper sensitivity, surface air-gulping behavior, diet recommendations, and temperature range broadly consistent with species-specific sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras acutus species profile
additional.notes: Additional confirmation of adult size (~5 cm), diagnostic black dorsal saddle, confusion with C. ellisae and C. amapaensis, breeding approach (T-position, rainy-season trigger), and hobbyist availability notes.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Hoplisoma reclassification note
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 Dias/Britto proposal to resurrect Hoplisoma as the senior synonym encompassing Corydoras acutus (proposed new combination: Hoplisoma acutus). Reclassification is pending broad adoption; Corydoras acutus retained as primary name per current hobby consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Blacktop Corydoras spawn using the T-position sequence typical of the genus, with two to three males courting each female. Spawning is commonly triggered by a partial water change with slightly cooler water. Females deposit adhesive eggs on flat surfaces such as glass or broad leaves; eggs should be moved to a separate hatching container. Fry accept micro-worms and fry powder once free-swimming.
